**Step 7: Web App Specific Requirements** — Progress: 7 of 13

Project type is `web_app`. Key questions from the configuration:

From context I already have strong answers to most of these. Let me confirm quickly:

- **SPA or MPA?** Static SPA — single Astro page, all charts render client-side
- **Browser support?** The demo will be recorded, so modern Chrome is sufficient — but should it work in Firefox/Safari too for live viewing?
- **SEO needed?** No — internal demo, not public
- **Real-time?** No — static cache, no live data updates
- **Accessibility?** Minimal — demo context, not a production tool

One question to confirm: **Does the dashboard need to be responsive / mobile-friendly**, or will it always be viewed on a desktop/laptop?

Got it. Here's the draft web app requirements:

---

## Web App Specific Requirements

### Project-Type Overview

Static single-page application built with Astro. All data loaded from a local JSON cache at build time. No backend, no authentication, no server-side rendering. Dashboard viewed on desktop during demo recording, but designed responsively for any team member who opens it in a browser.

### Technical Architecture Considerations

**Build Pipeline:**
- `npm run fetch-data` → `scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts` → writes `src/data/checkpoints-cache.json`
- `npm run build` → Astro static build reads cache at build time → outputs `dist/`
- `npm run dev` → dev server reads cache, hot-reload for dashboard development

**Data Flow:**
```
GitHub API (entire/checkpoints/v1)
  → fetch script (TypeScript, Node)
  → checkpoints-cache.json (local, gitignored)
  → Astro build (static)
  → browser (client-side chart rendering)
```

**Fetch Script Targets:**
- `GET /git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1` — discover all checkpoint paths
- `GET /contents/{path}/metadata.json` × ~41 files — parent + turn metadata
- `GET /commits?sha={branch}` — commit dates and checkpoint_id mapping
- Targeted `full.jsonl` parse — scan `type:"user"` messages for `/bmad-*` commands only

### Browser Matrix

| Browser | Support |
|---|---|
| Chrome (latest) | Primary — demo recording target |
| Firefox (latest) | Supported |
| Safari (latest) | Supported |
| Edge (latest) | Supported |
| Mobile browsers | Responsive layout, not primary use case |

### Responsive Design

- **Desktop (≥1024px):** 3–4 column card grid, full chart labels, sidebar summary stats
- **Tablet (768–1023px):** 2 column grid, condensed labels
- **Mobile (< 768px):** Single column, charts stack vertically — usable but not primary
- Chart library (lightweight-charts or similar) must support container-responsive sizing via `autoSize: true`

### Performance Targets

| Target | Value |
|---|---|
| Cache load time | < 2s from local JSON |
| `fetch-data` script | < 60s for ~44 API calls |
| JS bundle | < 150KB gzipped |
| First contentful paint | < 1.5s (local dev server) |
| Chart render (20 charts) | < 500ms after data load |

### SEO Strategy

None required — internal demo tool, no public indexing needed.

### Accessibility Level

WCAG AA for colour contrast on charts (green/red direction indicators, status colours). Semantic HTML for chart containers. No screen reader requirement for v1 demo scope.

### Implementation Considerations

- Charts: reuse existing `lightweight-charts` dependency already in the project, supplemented with a lightweight bar/pie chart library for non-time-series metrics (e.g., `Chart.js` or `unovis`)
- Styling: extend existing Tailwind v4 `@theme` tokens — dark crypto aesthetic already established in the repo
- No new routing needed — all 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page
- Cache file gitignored — developers run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`

# Product Requirements Document - AI Metrics Dashboard

**Author:** Dmytro
**Date:** 2026-03-26

## Executive Summary

Engineering teams adopting AI coding assistants face a structural blind spot: they can see the code that ships, but not how AI contributed to it, at what cost, with what friction, or at what quality risk. This dashboard is a proof-of-concept that demonstrates end-to-end AI usage observability for development teams, built entirely on data that Entire — a Git-native AI session capture platform — already produces on every commit.

The dashboard reads checkpoint data from a real project's `entire/checkpoints/v1` Git branch, processes it through a local JSON cache, and renders a comprehensive metrics view covering: code attribution (agent vs. human lines per commit), token economics (cost, cache efficiency, API call volume), quality signals (friction events, open items debt, human override rate), behavioral patterns (tool usage depth, session turn count, prompt classification), and temporal analytics (time-of-day heatmap, commit cadence, sprint-level trends).

**Target audience:** Engineering leads and platform teams evaluating AI observability tooling for internal SDLC integration.

**Objective:** Demonstrate — with real data from real work sessions — that Git-native AI metrics are feasible, valuable, and require no new data collection infrastructure beyond what Entire already provides.

### What Makes This Special

Entire captures AI session context as first-class versioned data in Git: transcripts, token usage, files touched, code attribution, friction events, and learnings — on every commit. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. No new tracking required, no agent modification, no external tooling integration. The data is already there.

The core insight: AI usage is the last untracked dimension of SDLC productivity. Every other dimension — velocity, quality, coverage, deployment frequency — has tooling. AI contribution, cost, and quality risk do not. Entire closes the capture gap; this dashboard closes the visibility gap.

Key differentiators:
- **Zero new infrastructure** — reads existing Git branch data via GitHub API
- **Attribution at line level** — agent_lines vs. human_modified vs. human_removed per commit
- **Quality-correlated signals** — friction, open items, and human override rate tied to individual checkpoints
- **Temporal filtering** — all metrics filterable and sortable by commit datetime for sprint-level and trend analysis

## Project Classification

| Dimension | Value |
|---|---|
| **Project Type** | Web application — static Astro SPA, client-side only |
| **Domain** | Internal engineering intelligence / developer tooling |
| **Complexity** | Medium — GitHub API integration, data pipeline, metrics computation, multi-card visualization |
| **Project Context** | Greenfield PoC — demonstration artifact for internal team evaluation |
| **Data Source** | `ShamsiievDmytro/entire-bmad`, branch `entire/checkpoints/v1` (hardcoded for demo) |
| **Future Path** | GitLab API migration, multi-repo support, real-time ingestion pipeline (out of v1 scope) |

## Success Criteria

### User Success

- An engineering lead reviewing the demo can answer these questions from the dashboard within 2 minutes: *How much of this codebase was written by AI? What did it cost in tokens? Where did friction occur? Which BMAD skills were used most?*
- The demo conveys the concept without verbal explanation — charts and numbers speak for themselves
- At least one stakeholder asks "can we apply this to our own repos?" — indicating the approach is understood as generalisable

### Business Success

- The team accepts Entire + this dashboard pattern as a viable approach for internal SDLC metrics integration
- The demo prompts a concrete next step: a pilot proposal, a request to Entire, or an internal spike
- The PoC demonstrates that zero new data collection infrastructure is needed — data already exists in Git

### Technical Success

- `npm run fetch-data` completes in under 60 seconds for the demo repo (~44 API calls + full.jsonl command extraction)
- Dashboard renders from local cache in under 2 seconds
- All 20 charts/metrics populate without errors from real `entire/checkpoints/v1` data
- No runtime errors during screen recording

### Measurable Outcomes

| Outcome | Signal |
|---|---|
| Demo is self-explanatory | No verbal explanation needed for any chart |
| Approach is credible | Team agrees data is real, sourced from actual work sessions |
| Scope is sufficient | All 4 metric domains represented (attribution, cost, quality, behavioral) |
| Follow-up happens | At least one concrete next step agreed in the meeting |

## Product Scope

### MVP — v1 Demo Dashboard (20 metrics across all domains)

**Data tiers:**
- **Lightweight** (metrics 1–13, 15–20): sourced from `metadata.json` files only (~150KB total)
- **Heavy** (metric 14): targeted parse of `full.jsonl` user messages for command extraction only

**Attribution (5 metrics)**
1. Agent % per commit — trend line over all checkpoints sorted by `commit_date`
2. Pure-AI commit rate — % of checkpoints where `agent_percentage === 100`
3. Human modification rate — `human_modified` lines as % of `agent_lines` per checkpoint
4. Agent vs. human lines — stacked bar per checkpoint (agent_lines / human_added / human_modified / human_removed)
5. Zero-human-touch lines total — cumulative `agent_lines` where all human attribution fields = 0

**Token Economics (4 metrics)**
6. Token breakdown per checkpoint — stacked: input / cache_creation / cache_read / output
7. Cache leverage score — `cache_read_tokens / total_tokens` per checkpoint, trended
8. Total API call count — per checkpoint bar chart
9. Model distribution — pie/donut of Claude models (`model` field) across all turns

**Quality Signals (4 metrics)**
10. Friction events per checkpoint — `summary.friction` array length, bar chart
11. Open items debt — `summary.open_items` array length per checkpoint, trend line
12. Learnings generated — sum of `learnings.repo + learnings.code + learnings.workflow` counts per checkpoint
13. Session depth — `session_metrics.turn_count` per turn, scatter against `agent_percentage`

**Behavioral / SDLC (4 metrics)**
14. BMAD command usage frequency — parse `type: "user"` messages in `full.jsonl` across all turns, extract all `/bmad-*` and `/*` command patterns, count and rank by frequency. Stored in cache as extracted command strings only. Source: heavy tier (`full.jsonl`)
15. Prompt type distribution — classify `prompt.txt` per turn as: slash command / free-form / continuation (empty), pie chart
16. Files touched per checkpoint — count, coloured by file layer (`components/`, `services/`, `stores/`, `utils/`, docs)
17. Checkpoints per session — group turns by `session_id`, show how many checkpoints each session spans

**Temporal (3 metrics)**
18. Commit activity timeline — all checkpoints on a time axis by `commit_date`, coloured by `agent_percentage`
19. Checkpoint cadence — time delta between consecutive commits in minutes, bar chart
20. Attribution trend over project — `agent_percentage` rolling average across all commits chronologically

### Growth Features (Post-MVP)

- Configurable repo/branch via UI or `.env`
- GitLab API support
- Multi-repo aggregation
- Per-developer breakdown
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, hook failure rate, stop reason distribution
- Export to CSV / shareable report

### Vision (Future)

- Real-time ingestion pipeline via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across multiple developers and repos
- DORA-style AI metrics benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal

## User Journeys

### Journey 1: The Presenter — Setting Up and Recording the Demo

**Persona:** Dmytro, developer evaluating AI observability tooling for his team. Has done research, needs a compelling recorded demo for a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** Day before the meeting. Runs `npm run fetch-data` — script walks the GitHub API checkpoint tree, fetches ~44 metadata files, parses `full.jsonl` for BMAD command patterns, writes `checkpoints-cache.json` in under 60 seconds.

**Rising Action:** Runs `npm run dev`, dashboard populates instantly from cache. Attribution trend shows arc from 60% to 100% agent-written code. BMAD command chart shows `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`. Token costs visible per checkpoint.

**Climax:** Screen recording — 4-minute walkthrough: attribution ("how much AI"), token economics ("what it cost"), quality signals ("where friction occurred"), BMAD commands ("which skills, how often").

**Resolution:** Concrete, data-driven artifact that needs no live infrastructure — just a browser and a video file.

**Capabilities revealed:** fetch script, local cache, static dashboard, all 20 metric charts, temporal ordering by commit date.

---

### Journey 2: The Viewer — Engineering Lead Evaluating the Approach

**Persona:** Senior engineering lead, skeptical of new tooling, interested in AI observability. Watches the recorded demo in a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** First question: *"Is this real data or a mock?"* Confirmed — real project, real commits, real sessions.

**Rising Action:** Attribution trend lands. *"That 100% agent line — zero human edits on that commit?"* Yes. *"Which files were touched?"* Files-by-layer chart answers it. *"Can we see if AI touches services differently than components?"* Yes.

**Climax:** BMAD command frequency chart appears. `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`, `/bmad-create-story ×3`. *"This shows exactly how the developer structured their AI workflow — not just that they used AI, but how."* The approach clicks.

**Resolution:** *"Can we run this against our own repos?"* — the success signal. Team agrees to evaluate Entire as a platform.

**Capabilities revealed:** data credibility indicator, commit date ordering, files-by-layer chart, BMAD command breakdown, clear chart labels.

---

### Journey 3: The Sceptic — Developer Who Questions the Data

**Persona:** Developer on the team concerned that AI metrics could be used to evaluate individual performance.

**Opening Scene:** Sees attribution charts. *"Is this going to be used to judge how much AI someone uses?"*

**Rising Action:** Dashboard shows aggregate patterns — no developer names, no performance ranking. Quality signals show where the workflow struggled, not who struggled.

**Resolution:** The data model itself answers the trust question — session-level context only, no PII, no individual performance ranking.

**Capabilities revealed:** no PII/developer identification in v1, aggregate-only view, metrics framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs.

---

### Journey Requirements Summary

| Capability | Revealed by Journey |
|---|---|
| `npm run fetch-data` script | J1 |
| Local JSON cache, sub-2s load | J1 |
| All 20 metric charts | J1, J2 |
| Commit date ordering / temporal axis | J1, J2 |
| Files touched by layer chart | J2 |
| BMAD command frequency chart | J2 |
| Clear chart labels, no jargon | J2 |
| No PII, aggregate-only model | J3 |
| Data source credibility indicator | J2, J3 |

## Innovation & Novel Patterns

### Detected Innovation Areas

**1. Git as AI Observability Store**
Entire repurposes Git — already universal in engineering teams — as the persistence layer for AI session telemetry. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. The innovation is not in the dashboard itself but in the data model it reads: structured, versioned, permanently associated with the commit it produced.

**2. Zero-Infrastructure Observability**
Most observability tools require agents, sidecars, log shippers, or SaaS integrations. This approach requires none — the data pipeline is `git push`. The dashboard is a read layer on top of data that already exists. Category inversion: instead of "add instrumentation to capture AI usage," it's "read the instrumentation that AI commits already carry."

**3. Attribution at Commit Granularity**
Existing tools (GitHub Copilot metrics, IDE plugins) report AI usage at session or daily level. Entire captures attribution per commit — `agent_lines` vs `human_modified` vs `human_removed` per checkpoint. This dashboard is the first visualisation layer for that granularity.

**4. Workflow Intelligence from Prompt Patterns**
Parsing `/bmad-*` command invocations from user messages surfaces *how* AI was directed, not just *that* it was used. New signal category: AI workflow structure, visible in aggregate across a project's history.

### Validation Approach

The PoC itself is the validation — real data from a real project (`entire/checkpoints/v1`) running through the pipeline. Secondary validation: the team asks "can we run this on our own repos?" — that question confirms the approach generalises.

### Risk Mitigation

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Sparse checkpoint data | Demo uses a completed project with 11 checkpoints — sufficient density |
| GitHub API rate limits | ~44 calls, within unauthenticated 60/hour limit; token optional |
| GitLab migration breaks approach | Abstract fetch layer; GitHub/GitLab interchangeable at API level |
| Team perceives as surveillance | No PII, aggregate-only, framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs |

## Web App Specific Requirements

### Project-Type Overview

Static single-page application built with Astro. All data loaded from a local JSON cache at build time. No backend, no authentication, no server-side rendering. Dashboard viewed on desktop during demo recording, designed responsively for any team member who opens it in a browser.

### Technical Architecture Considerations

**Build Pipeline:**
```
npm run fetch-data
  → scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts
  → src/data/checkpoints-cache.json (local, gitignored)

npm run dev / build
  → Astro reads cache at build time
  → client-side chart rendering
```

**Fetch Script Targets:**
- `GET /git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1` — discover all checkpoint paths
- `GET /contents/{path}/metadata.json` × ~41 files — parent + turn metadata
- `GET /commits?sha={branch}` — commit dates and checkpoint_id mapping
- Targeted `full.jsonl` parse — scan `type:"user"` messages for `/bmad-*` commands only

### Browser Matrix

| Browser | Support |
|---|---|
| Chrome (latest) | Primary — demo recording target |
| Firefox (latest) | Supported |
| Safari (latest) | Supported |
| Edge (latest) | Supported |
| Mobile browsers | Responsive layout, not primary use case |

### Responsive Design

- **Desktop (≥1024px):** 3–4 column card grid, full chart labels, summary stats
- **Tablet (768–1023px):** 2 column grid, condensed labels
- **Mobile (<768px):** Single column, charts stack vertically — usable but not primary
- Chart library must support container-responsive sizing via `autoSize: true`

### Performance Targets

| Target | Value |
|---|---|
| Cache load time | < 2s from local JSON |
| `fetch-data` script | < 60s for ~44 API calls + full.jsonl command scan |
| JS bundle | < 150KB gzipped |
| First contentful paint | < 1.5s (local dev server) |
| Chart render (20 charts) | < 500ms after data load |

### SEO Strategy

None required — internal demo, no public indexing.

### Accessibility Level

WCAG AA colour contrast on charts. Semantic HTML for chart containers. No screen reader requirement for v1 demo scope.

### Implementation Considerations

- Charts: reuse existing `lightweight-charts` for time-series; add lightweight bar/pie library (e.g., `Chart.js` or `unovis`) for non-time-series metrics
- Styling: extend existing Tailwind v4 `@theme` tokens — dark aesthetic already established
- All 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page — no routing needed
- Cache file gitignored — run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`

11. Open items debt — `summary.open_items` array length per checkpoint, trend line
12. Learnings generated — sum of `learnings.repo + learnings.code + learnings.workflow` counts per checkpoint
13. Session depth — `session_metrics.turn_count` per turn, scatter against `agent_percentage`

**Behavioral / SDLC (4 metrics)**
14. BMAD command usage frequency — parse `type: "user"` messages in `full.jsonl` across all turns, extract all `/bmad-*` and `/*` command patterns, count and rank by frequency. Stored in cache as extracted command strings only. Source: heavy tier (`full.jsonl`)
15. Prompt type distribution — classify `prompt.txt` per turn as: slash command / free-form / continuation (empty), pie chart
16. Files touched per checkpoint — count, coloured by file layer (`components/`, `services/`, `stores/`, `utils/`, docs)
17. Checkpoints per session — group turns by `session_id`, show how many checkpoints each session spans

**Temporal (3 metrics)**
18. Commit activity timeline — all checkpoints on a time axis by `commit_date`, coloured by `agent_percentage`
19. Checkpoint cadence — time delta between consecutive commits in minutes, bar chart
20. Attribution trend over project — `agent_percentage` rolling average across all commits chronologically

### Growth Features (Post-MVP)

- Configurable repo/branch via UI or `.env`
- GitLab API support
- Multi-repo aggregation
- Per-developer breakdown
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, hook failure rate, stop reason distribution
- Export to CSV / shareable report

### Vision (Future)

- Real-time ingestion pipeline via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across multiple developers and repos
- DORA-style AI metrics benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal

## User Journeys

### Journey 1: The Presenter — Setting Up and Recording the Demo

**Persona:** Dmytro, developer evaluating AI observability tooling for his team. Has done research, needs a compelling recorded demo for a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** Day before the meeting. Runs `npm run fetch-data` — script walks the GitHub API checkpoint tree, fetches ~44 metadata files, parses `full.jsonl` for BMAD command patterns, writes `checkpoints-cache.json` in under 60 seconds.

**Rising Action:** Runs `npm run dev`, dashboard populates instantly from cache. Attribution trend shows arc from 60% to 100% agent-written code. BMAD command chart shows `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`. Token costs visible per checkpoint.

**Climax:** Screen recording — 4-minute walkthrough: attribution ("how much AI"), token economics ("what it cost"), quality signals ("where friction occurred"), BMAD commands ("which skills, how often").

**Resolution:** Concrete, data-driven artifact that needs no live infrastructure — just a browser and a video file.

**Capabilities revealed:** fetch script, local cache, static dashboard, all 20 metric charts, temporal ordering by commit date.

---

### Journey 2: The Viewer — Engineering Lead Evaluating the Approach

**Persona:** Senior engineering lead, skeptical of new tooling, interested in AI observability. Watches the recorded demo in a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** First question: *"Is this real data or a mock?"* Confirmed — real project, real commits, real sessions.

**Rising Action:** Attribution trend lands. *"That 100% agent line — zero human edits on that commit?"* Yes. *"Which files were touched?"* Files-by-layer chart answers it. *"Can we see if AI touches services differently than components?"* Yes.

**Climax:** BMAD command frequency chart appears. `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`, `/bmad-create-story ×3`. *"This shows exactly how the developer structured their AI workflow — not just that they used AI, but how."* The approach clicks.

**Resolution:** *"Can we run this against our own repos?"* — the success signal. Team agrees to evaluate Entire as a platform.

**Capabilities revealed:** data credibility indicator, commit date ordering, files-by-layer chart, BMAD command breakdown, clear chart labels.

---

### Journey 3: The Sceptic — Developer Who Questions the Data

**Persona:** Developer on the team concerned that AI metrics could be used to evaluate individual performance.

**Opening Scene:** Sees attribution charts. *"Is this going to be used to judge how much AI someone uses?"*

**Rising Action:** Dashboard shows aggregate patterns — no developer names, no performance ranking. Quality signals show where the workflow struggled, not who struggled.

**Resolution:** The data model itself answers the trust question — session-level context only, no PII, no individual performance ranking.

**Capabilities revealed:** no PII/developer identification in v1, aggregate-only view, metrics framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs.

---

### Journey Requirements Summary

| Capability | Revealed by Journey |
|---|---|
| `npm run fetch-data` script | J1 |
| Local JSON cache, sub-2s load | J1 |
| All 20 metric charts | J1, J2 |
| Commit date ordering / temporal axis | J1, J2 |
| Files touched by layer chart | J2 |
| BMAD command frequency chart | J2 |
| Clear chart labels, no jargon | J2 |
| No PII, aggregate-only model | J3 |
| Data source credibility indicator | J2, J3 |

## Innovation & Novel Patterns

### Detected Innovation Areas

**1. Git as AI Observability Store**
Entire repurposes Git — already universal in engineering teams — as the persistence layer for AI session telemetry. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. The innovation is not in the dashboard itself but in the data model it reads: structured, versioned, permanently associated with the commit it produced.

**2. Zero-Infrastructure Observability**
Most observability tools require agents, sidecars, log shippers, or SaaS integrations. This approach requires none — the data pipeline is `git push`. The dashboard is a read layer on top of data that already exists. Category inversion: instead of "add instrumentation to capture AI usage," it's "read the instrumentation that AI commits already carry."

**3. Attribution at Commit Granularity**
Existing tools (GitHub Copilot metrics, IDE plugins) report AI usage at session or daily level. Entire captures attribution per commit — `agent_lines` vs `human_modified` vs `human_removed` per checkpoint. This dashboard is the first visualisation layer for that granularity.

**4. Workflow Intelligence from Prompt Patterns**
Parsing `/bmad-*` command invocations from user messages surfaces *how* AI was directed, not just *that* it was used. New signal category: AI workflow structure, visible in aggregate across a project's history.

### Validation Approach

The PoC itself is the validation — real data from a real project (`entire/checkpoints/v1`) running through the pipeline. Secondary validation: the team asks "can we run this on our own repos?" — that question confirms the approach generalises.

### Risk Mitigation

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Sparse checkpoint data | Demo uses a completed project with 11 checkpoints — sufficient density |
| GitHub API rate limits | ~44 calls, within unauthenticated 60/hour limit; token optional |
| GitLab migration breaks approach | Abstract fetch layer; GitHub/GitLab interchangeable at API level |
| Team perceives as surveillance | No PII, aggregate-only, framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs |

lines: | GitLab migration breaks approach | Abstract fetch layer; GitHub/GitLab interchangeable at API level |
| Team perceives as surveillance | No PII, aggregate-only, framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs |
+## Web App Specific Requirements
+### Project-Type Overview

+Static single-page application built with Astro. All data loaded from a local JSON cache at build time. No backend, no authentication, no server-side rendering. Dashboard viewed on desktop during demo recording, designed responsively for any team member who opens it in a browser.
+### Technical Architecture Considerations
+**Build Pipeline:**
+npm run fetch-data
+  → scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts
+  → src/data/checkpoints-cache.json (local, gitignored)
+npm run dev / build
+  → Astro reads cache at build time
+  → client-side chart rendering
+**Fetch Script Targets:**
+- `GET /git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1` — discover all checkpoint paths
+- `GET /contents/{path}/metadata.json` × ~41 files — parent + turn metadata
+- `GET /commits?sha={branch}` — commit dates and checkpoint_id mapping
+- Targeted `full.jsonl` parse — scan `type:"user"` messages for `/bmad-*` commands only
+### Browser Matrix
+| Browser | Support |
+| Chrome (latest) | Primary — demo recording target |
+| Firefox (latest) | Supported |
+| Safari (latest) | Supported |
+| Edge (latest) | Supported |
+| Mobile browsers | Responsive layout, not primary use case |
+### Responsive Design
+- **Desktop (≥1024px):** 3–4 column card grid, full chart labels, summary stats
+- **Tablet (768–1023px):** 2 column grid, condensed labels
+- **Mobile (<768px):** Single column, charts stack vertically — usable but not primary
+- Chart library must support container-responsive sizing via `autoSize: true`
+### Performance Targets
+| Target | Value |
+| Cache load time | < 2s from local JSON |
+| `fetch-data` script | < 60s for ~44 API calls + full.jsonl command scan |
+| JS bundle | < 150KB gzipped |
+| First contentful paint | < 1.5s (local dev server) |
+| Chart render (20 charts) | < 500ms after data load |
+### SEO Strategy
+None required — internal demo, no public indexing.
+### Accessibility Level
+WCAG AA colour contrast on charts. Semantic HTML for chart containers. No screen reader requirement for v1 demo scope.
+### Implementation Considerations
+- Charts: reuse existing `lightweight-charts` for time-series; add lightweight bar/pie library (e.g., `Chart.js` or `unovis`) for non-time-series metrics
+- Styling: extend existing Tailwind v4 `@theme` tokens — dark aesthetic already established
+- All 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page — no routing needed
+- Cache file gitignored — run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`

# Step 8: Scoping Exercise - MVP & Future Features

**Progress: Step 8 of 11** - Next: Functional Requirements

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

- 🛑 NEVER generate content without user input

- 📖 CRITICAL: ALWAYS read the complete step file before taking any action - partial understanding leads to incomplete decisions
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step with 'C', ensure the entire file is read and understood before proceeding
- ✅ ALWAYS treat this as collaborative discovery between PM peers
- 📋 YOU ARE A FACILITATOR, not a content generator
- 💬 FOCUS on strategic scope decisions that keep projects viable
- 🎯 EMPHASIZE lean MVP thinking while preserving long-term vision
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS WRITE all artifact and document content in `{document_output_language}`

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Show your analysis before taking any action
- 📚 Review the complete PRD document built so far
- ⚠️ Present A/P/C menu after generating scoping decisions
- 💾 ONLY save when user chooses C (Continue)
- 📖 Update output file frontmatter, adding this step name to the end of the list of stepsCompleted
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to load next step until C is selected


## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Complete PRD document built so far is available for review
- User journeys, success criteria, and domain requirements are documented
- Focus on strategic scope decisions, not feature details
- Balance between user value and implementation feasibility

## YOUR TASK:

Conduct comprehensive scoping exercise to define MVP boundaries and prioritize features across development phases.

## SCOPING SEQUENCE:

### 1. Review Current PRD State

Analyze everything documented so far:
- Present synthesis of established vision, success criteria, journeys
- Assess domain and innovation focus
- Evaluate scope implications: simple MVP, medium, or complex project
- Ask if initial assessment feels right or if they see it differently

### 2. Define MVP Strategy

Facilitate strategic MVP decisions:
- Explore MVP philosophy options: problem-solving, experience, platform, or revenue MVP
- Ask critical questions:
  - What's the minimum that would make users say 'this is useful'?
  - What would make investors/partners say 'this has potential'?
  - What's the fastest path to validated learning?
- Guide toward appropriate MVP approach for their product

### 3. Scoping Decision Framework

Use structured decision-making for scope:

**Must-Have Analysis:**
- Guide identification of absolute MVP necessities
- For each journey and success criterion, ask:
  - Without this, does the product fail?
  - Can this be manual initially?
  - Is this a deal-breaker for early adopters?
- Analyze journeys for MVP essentials

**Nice-to-Have Analysis:**
- Identify what could be added later:
  - Features that enhance but aren't essential
  - User types that can be added later
  - Advanced functionality that builds on MVP
- Ask what features could be added in versions 2, 3, etc.

### 4. Progressive Feature Roadmap

Create phased development approach:
- Guide mapping of features across development phases
- Structure as Phase 1 (MVP), Phase 2 (Growth), Phase 3 (Vision)
- Ensure clear progression and dependencies

- Core user value delivery
- Essential user journeys
- Basic functionality that works reliably

**Phase 2: Growth**

- Additional user types
- Enhanced features
- Scale improvements

**Phase 3: Expansion**

- Advanced capabilities
- Platform features
- New markets or use cases

**Where does your current vision fit in this development sequence?**"

### 5. Risk-Based Scoping

Identify and mitigate scoping risks:

**Technical Risks:**
"Looking at your innovation and domain requirements:

- What's the most technically challenging aspect?
- Could we simplify the initial implementation?
- What's the riskiest assumption about technology feasibility?"

**Market Risks:**

- What's the biggest market risk?
- How does the MVP address this?
- What learning do we need to de-risk this?"

**Resource Risks:**

- What if we have fewer resources than planned?
- What's the absolute minimum team size needed?
- Can we launch with a smaller feature set?"

### 6. Generate Scoping Content

Prepare comprehensive scoping section:

#### Content Structure:

```markdown
## Project Scoping & Phased Development

### MVP Strategy & Philosophy

**MVP Approach:** {{chosen_mvp_approach}}
**Resource Requirements:** {{mvp_team_size_and_skills}}

### MVP Feature Set (Phase 1)

**Core User Journeys Supported:**
{{essential_journeys_for_mvp}}

**Must-Have Capabilities:**
{{list_of_essential_mvp_features}}

### Post-MVP Features

**Phase 2 (Post-MVP):**
{{planned_growth_features}}

**Phase 3 (Expansion):**
{{planned_expansion_features}}

### Risk Mitigation Strategy

**Technical Risks:** {{mitigation_approach}}
**Market Risks:** {{validation_approach}}
**Resource Risks:** {{contingency_approach}}
```

### 7. Present MENU OPTIONS

Present the scoping decisions for review, then display menu:
- Show strategic scoping plan (using structure from step 6)
- Highlight MVP boundaries and phased roadmap
- Ask if they'd like to refine further, get other perspectives, or proceed
- Present menu options naturally as part of conversation

Display: "**Select:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Functional Requirements (Step 9 of 11)"

#### Menu Handling Logic:
- IF A: Invoke the `bmad-advanced-elicitation` skill with the current scoping analysis, process the enhanced insights that come back, ask user if they accept the improvements, if yes update content then redisplay menu, if no keep original content then redisplay menu
- IF P: Invoke the `bmad-party-mode` skill with the scoping context, process the collaborative insights on MVP and roadmap decisions, ask user if they accept the changes, if yes update content then redisplay menu, if no keep original content then redisplay menu
- IF C: Append the final content to {outputFile}, update frontmatter by adding this step name to the end of the stepsCompleted array, then read fully and follow: ./step-09-functional.md
- IF Any other: help user respond, then redisplay menu

#### EXECUTION RULES:
- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'
- After other menu items execution, return to this menu

## APPEND TO DOCUMENT:

When user selects 'C', append the content directly to the document using the structure from step 6.

## SUCCESS METRICS:

✅ Complete PRD document analyzed for scope implications
✅ Strategic MVP approach defined and justified
✅ Clear MVP feature boundaries established
✅ Phased development roadmap created
✅ Key risks identified and mitigation strategies defined
✅ User explicitly agrees to scope decisions
✅ A/P/C menu presented and handled correctly
✅ Content properly appended to document when C selected

## FAILURE MODES:

❌ Not analyzing the complete PRD before making scoping decisions
❌ Making scope decisions without strategic rationale
❌ Not getting explicit user agreement on MVP boundaries
❌ Missing critical risk analysis
❌ Not creating clear phased development approach
❌ Not presenting A/P/C menu after content generation

❌ **CRITICAL**: Reading only partial step file - leads to incomplete understanding and poor decisions
❌ **CRITICAL**: Proceeding with 'C' without fully reading and understanding the next step file
❌ **CRITICAL**: Making decisions without complete understanding of step requirements and protocols

## NEXT STEP:

After user selects 'C' and content is saved to document, load ./step-09-functional.md.

Remember: Do NOT proceed to step-09 until user explicitly selects 'C' from the A/P/C menu and content is saved!

**Step 8: Scoping** — Progress: 8 of 13

Reviewing the full PRD state — scope is already well-established from our earlier work. Let me synthesise it into the scoping structure and confirm there are no gaps.

**Current MVP assessment:** Lean demo PoC. One developer (you), one repo, one sprint to build. The fetch script + cache + 20 charts is the entire surface area. No scope creep risk given the demo-only constraint.

**MVP Philosophy:** *Problem-solving MVP* — the minimum that makes the concept undeniable. All 20 metrics are in scope because each one represents a distinct signal type the team needs to see to evaluate the approach.

**Technical risk:** The only non-trivial risk is the `full.jsonl` parse for BMAD commands (metric #14) — large files, targeted scan. Mitigated by extracting only command strings, not full transcript content.

Here's the scoping section:

---

## Project Scoping & Phased Development

### MVP Strategy & Philosophy

**MVP Approach:** Problem-solving PoC — demonstrate that Git-native AI observability is real, functional, and meaningful using actual checkpoint data from a completed project.

**Resource Requirements:** 1 developer, ~1 sprint. No external dependencies beyond GitHub API (unauthenticated) and existing Astro repo infrastructure.

### MVP Feature Set (Phase 1) — v1 Demo Dashboard

**Core User Journeys Supported:**
- Journey 1: Presenter runs fetch, records dashboard walkthrough
- Journey 2: Viewer evaluates the approach from the recording
- Journey 3: Sceptic's trust concern addressed by aggregate-only data model

**Must-Have Capabilities:**

| Capability | Justification |
|---|---|
| `scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts` | Entire data pipeline — nothing works without it |
| `src/data/checkpoints-cache.json` schema | Contracts between fetch script and dashboard |
| 20 metric charts (all domains) | Each represents a distinct signal category for evaluation |
| Temporal ordering by `commit_date` | Required for trend analysis |
| BMAD command frequency (metric #14) | Key workflow intelligence signal — requires `full.jsonl` parse |
| Responsive layout | Team members may open on different devices |
| Data source credibility indicator | Addresses the "is this real?" question immediately |

**Out of MVP Scope (explicitly):**
- Configurable repo/branch — hardcoded to `ShamsiievDmytro/entire-bmad`
- Authentication — public repo, no token required for demo
- Multiple pages or routing — single scrollable page only
- Export / share features

### Phase 2 — Growth (Post-Demo, if adopted internally)

- `.env`-configurable repo owner, repo name, branch
- GitLab API support alongside GitHub API
- Multi-repo aggregation view
- Per-developer breakdown (requires multi-developer checkpoint data)
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, stop reason distribution
- CSV/report export

### Phase 3 — Expansion (Platform Vision)

- Real-time ingestion via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across developers and repos
- DORA-style AI benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal or engineering dashboard

### Risk Mitigation Strategy

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **Technical:** `full.jsonl` files too large for targeted parse | Stream line-by-line; extract command strings only; skip on error |
| **Technical:** GitHub API 60 req/hour unauthenticated limit | ~44 calls — within limit; optional `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var for headroom |
| **Scope:** 20 charts feels like too many for one page | Group into tabbed sections or collapsible domain panels |
| **Demo:** Team asks for live data during meeting | Recorded walkthrough removes live dependency; cache can be refreshed anytime |
| **Adoption:** GitLab-only teams can't adopt | Architecture note in PRD; GitLab migration is Phase 2 priority |

# Product Requirements Document - AI Metrics Dashboard

**Author:** Dmytro
**Date:** 2026-03-26

## Executive Summary

Engineering teams adopting AI coding assistants face a structural blind spot: they can see the code that ships, but not how AI contributed to it, at what cost, with what friction, or at what quality risk. This dashboard is a proof-of-concept that demonstrates end-to-end AI usage observability for development teams, built entirely on data that Entire — a Git-native AI session capture platform — already produces on every commit.

The dashboard reads checkpoint data from a real project's `entire/checkpoints/v1` Git branch, processes it through a local JSON cache, and renders a comprehensive metrics view covering: code attribution (agent vs. human lines per commit), token economics (cost, cache efficiency, API call volume), quality signals (friction events, open items debt, human override rate), behavioral patterns (tool usage depth, session turn count, prompt classification), and temporal analytics (time-of-day heatmap, commit cadence, sprint-level trends).

**Target audience:** Engineering leads and platform teams evaluating AI observability tooling for internal SDLC integration.

**Objective:** Demonstrate — with real data from real work sessions — that Git-native AI metrics are feasible, valuable, and require no new data collection infrastructure beyond what Entire already provides.

### What Makes This Special

Entire captures AI session context as first-class versioned data in Git: transcripts, token usage, files touched, code attribution, friction events, and learnings — on every commit. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. No new tracking required, no agent modification, no external tooling integration. The data is already there.

The core insight: AI usage is the last untracked dimension of SDLC productivity. Every other dimension — velocity, quality, coverage, deployment frequency — has tooling. AI contribution, cost, and quality risk do not. Entire closes the capture gap; this dashboard closes the visibility gap.

Key differentiators:
- **Zero new infrastructure** — reads existing Git branch data via GitHub API
- **Attribution at line level** — agent_lines vs. human_modified vs. human_removed per commit
- **Quality-correlated signals** — friction, open items, and human override rate tied to individual checkpoints
- **Temporal filtering** — all metrics filterable and sortable by commit datetime for sprint-level and trend analysis

## Project Classification

| Dimension | Value |
|---|---|
| **Project Type** | Web application — static Astro SPA, client-side only |
| **Domain** | Internal engineering intelligence / developer tooling |
| **Complexity** | Medium — GitHub API integration, data pipeline, metrics computation, multi-card visualization |
| **Project Context** | Greenfield PoC — demonstration artifact for internal team evaluation |
| **Data Source** | `ShamsiievDmytro/entire-bmad`, branch `entire/checkpoints/v1` (hardcoded for demo) |
| **Future Path** | GitLab API migration, multi-repo support, real-time ingestion pipeline (out of v1 scope) |

## Success Criteria

### User Success

- An engineering lead reviewing the demo can answer these questions from the dashboard within 2 minutes: *How much of this codebase was written by AI? What did it cost in tokens? Where did friction occur? Which BMAD skills were used most?*
- The demo conveys the concept without verbal explanation — charts and numbers speak for themselves
- At least one stakeholder asks "can we apply this to our own repos?" — indicating the approach is understood as generalisable

### Business Success

- The team accepts Entire + this dashboard pattern as a viable approach for internal SDLC metrics integration
- The demo prompts a concrete next step: a pilot proposal, a request to Entire, or an internal spike
- The PoC demonstrates that zero new data collection infrastructure is needed — data already exists in Git

### Technical Success

- `npm run fetch-data` completes in under 60 seconds for the demo repo (~44 API calls + full.jsonl command extraction)
- Dashboard renders from local cache in under 2 seconds
- All 20 charts/metrics populate without errors from real `entire/checkpoints/v1` data
- No runtime errors during screen recording

### Measurable Outcomes

| Outcome | Signal |
|---|---|
| Demo is self-explanatory | No verbal explanation needed for any chart |
| Approach is credible | Team agrees data is real, sourced from actual work sessions |
| Scope is sufficient | All 4 metric domains represented (attribution, cost, quality, behavioral) |
| Follow-up happens | At least one concrete next step agreed in the meeting |

## Product Scope

### MVP — v1 Demo Dashboard (20 metrics across all domains)

**Data tiers:**
- **Lightweight** (metrics 1–13, 15–20): sourced from `metadata.json` files only (~150KB total)
- **Heavy** (metric 14): targeted parse of `full.jsonl` user messages for command extraction only

**Attribution (5 metrics)**
1. Agent % per commit — trend line over all checkpoints sorted by `commit_date`
2. Pure-AI commit rate — % of checkpoints where `agent_percentage === 100`
3. Human modification rate — `human_modified` lines as % of `agent_lines` per checkpoint
4. Agent vs. human lines — stacked bar per checkpoint (agent_lines / human_added / human_modified / human_removed)
5. Zero-human-touch lines total — cumulative `agent_lines` where all human attribution fields = 0

**Token Economics (4 metrics)**
6. Token breakdown per checkpoint — stacked: input / cache_creation / cache_read / output
7. Cache leverage score — `cache_read_tokens / total_tokens` per checkpoint, trended
8. Total API call count — per checkpoint bar chart
9. Model distribution — pie/donut of Claude models (`model` field) across all turns

**Quality Signals (4 metrics)**
10. Friction events per checkpoint — `summary.friction` array length, bar chart
11. Open items debt — `summary.open_items` array length per checkpoint, trend line
12. Learnings generated — sum of `learnings.repo + learnings.code + learnings.workflow` counts per checkpoint
13. Session depth — `session_metrics.turn_count` per turn, scatter against `agent_percentage`

**Behavioral / SDLC (4 metrics)**
14. BMAD command usage frequency — parse `type: "user"` messages in `full.jsonl` across all turns, extract all `/bmad-*` and `/*` command patterns, count and rank by frequency. Stored in cache as extracted command strings only. Source: heavy tier (`full.jsonl`)
15. Prompt type distribution — classify `prompt.txt` per turn as: slash command / free-form / continuation (empty), pie chart
16. Files touched per checkpoint — count, coloured by file layer (`components/`, `services/`, `stores/`, `utils/`, docs)
17. Checkpoints per session — group turns by `session_id`, show how many checkpoints each session spans

**Temporal (3 metrics)**
18. Commit activity timeline — all checkpoints on a time axis by `commit_date`, coloured by `agent_percentage`
19. Checkpoint cadence — time delta between consecutive commits in minutes, bar chart
20. Attribution trend over project — `agent_percentage` rolling average across all commits chronologically

### Growth Features (Post-MVP)

- Configurable repo/branch via UI or `.env`
- GitLab API support
- Multi-repo aggregation
- Per-developer breakdown
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, hook failure rate, stop reason distribution
- Export to CSV / shareable report

### Vision (Future)

- Real-time ingestion pipeline via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across multiple developers and repos
- DORA-style AI metrics benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal

## User Journeys

### Journey 1: The Presenter — Setting Up and Recording the Demo

**Persona:** Dmytro, developer evaluating AI observability tooling for his team. Has done research, needs a compelling recorded demo for a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** Day before the meeting. Runs `npm run fetch-data` — script walks the GitHub API checkpoint tree, fetches ~44 metadata files, parses `full.jsonl` for BMAD command patterns, writes `checkpoints-cache.json` in under 60 seconds.

**Rising Action:** Runs `npm run dev`, dashboard populates instantly from cache. Attribution trend shows arc from 60% to 100% agent-written code. BMAD command chart shows `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`. Token costs visible per checkpoint.

**Climax:** Screen recording — 4-minute walkthrough: attribution ("how much AI"), token economics ("what it cost"), quality signals ("where friction occurred"), BMAD commands ("which skills, how often").

**Resolution:** Concrete, data-driven artifact that needs no live infrastructure — just a browser and a video file.

**Capabilities revealed:** fetch script, local cache, static dashboard, all 20 metric charts, temporal ordering by commit date.

---

### Journey 2: The Viewer — Engineering Lead Evaluating the Approach

**Persona:** Senior engineering lead, skeptical of new tooling, interested in AI observability. Watches the recorded demo in a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** First question: *"Is this real data or a mock?"* Confirmed — real project, real commits, real sessions.

**Rising Action:** Attribution trend lands. *"That 100% agent line — zero human edits on that commit?"* Yes. *"Which files were touched?"* Files-by-layer chart answers it. *"Can we see if AI touches services differently than components?"* Yes.

**Climax:** BMAD command frequency chart appears. `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`, `/bmad-create-story ×3`. *"This shows exactly how the developer structured their AI workflow — not just that they used AI, but how."* The approach clicks.

**Resolution:** *"Can we run this against our own repos?"* — the success signal. Team agrees to evaluate Entire as a platform.

**Capabilities revealed:** data credibility indicator, commit date ordering, files-by-layer chart, BMAD command breakdown, clear chart labels.

---

### Journey 3: The Sceptic — Developer Who Questions the Data

**Persona:** Developer on the team concerned that AI metrics could be used to evaluate individual performance.

**Opening Scene:** Sees attribution charts. *"Is this going to be used to judge how much AI someone uses?"*

**Rising Action:** Dashboard shows aggregate patterns — no developer names, no performance ranking. Quality signals show where the workflow struggled, not who struggled.

**Resolution:** The data model itself answers the trust question — session-level context only, no PII, no individual performance ranking.

**Capabilities revealed:** no PII/developer identification in v1, aggregate-only view, metrics framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs.

---

### Journey Requirements Summary

| Capability | Revealed by Journey |
|---|---|
| `npm run fetch-data` script | J1 |
| Local JSON cache, sub-2s load | J1 |
| All 20 metric charts | J1, J2 |
| Commit date ordering / temporal axis | J1, J2 |
| Files touched by layer chart | J2 |
| BMAD command frequency chart | J2 |
| Clear chart labels, no jargon | J2 |
| No PII, aggregate-only model | J3 |
| Data source credibility indicator | J2, J3 |

## Innovation & Novel Patterns

### Detected Innovation Areas

**1. Git as AI Observability Store**
Entire repurposes Git — already universal in engineering teams — as the persistence layer for AI session telemetry. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. The innovation is not in the dashboard itself but in the data model it reads: structured, versioned, permanently associated with the commit it produced.

**2. Zero-Infrastructure Observability**
Most observability tools require agents, sidecars, log shippers, or SaaS integrations. This approach requires none — the data pipeline is `git push`. The dashboard is a read layer on top of data that already exists. Category inversion: instead of "add instrumentation to capture AI usage," it's "read the instrumentation that AI commits already carry."

**3. Attribution at Commit Granularity**
Existing tools (GitHub Copilot metrics, IDE plugins) report AI usage at session or daily level. Entire captures attribution per commit — `agent_lines` vs `human_modified` vs `human_removed` per checkpoint. This dashboard is the first visualisation layer for that granularity.

**4. Workflow Intelligence from Prompt Patterns**
Parsing `/bmad-*` command invocations from user messages surfaces *how* AI was directed, not just *that* it was used. New signal category: AI workflow structure, visible in aggregate across a project's history.

### Validation Approach

The PoC itself is the validation — real data from a real project (`entire/checkpoints/v1`) running through the pipeline. Secondary validation: the team asks "can we run this on our own repos?" — that question confirms the approach generalises.

### Risk Mitigation

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Sparse checkpoint data | Demo uses a completed project with 11 checkpoints — sufficient density |
| GitHub API rate limits | ~44 calls, within unauthenticated 60/hour limit; token optional |
| GitLab migration breaks approach | Abstract fetch layer; GitHub/GitLab interchangeable at API level |
| Team perceives as surveillance | No PII, aggregate-only, framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs |

## Web App Specific Requirements

### Project-Type Overview

Static single-page application built with Astro. All data loaded from a local JSON cache at build time. No backend, no authentication, no server-side rendering. Dashboard viewed on desktop during demo recording, designed responsively for any team member who opens it in a browser.

### Technical Architecture Considerations

**Build Pipeline:**
```
npm run fetch-data
  → scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts
  → src/data/checkpoints-cache.json (local, gitignored)

npm run dev / build
  → Astro reads cache at build time
  → client-side chart rendering
```

**Fetch Script Targets:**
- `GET /git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1` — discover all checkpoint paths
- `GET /contents/{path}/metadata.json` × ~41 files — parent + turn metadata
- `GET /commits?sha={branch}` — commit dates and checkpoint_id mapping
- Targeted `full.jsonl` parse — scan `type:"user"` messages for `/bmad-*` commands only

### Browser Matrix

| Browser | Support |
|---|---|
| Chrome (latest) | Primary — demo recording target |
| Firefox (latest) | Supported |
| Safari (latest) | Supported |
| Edge (latest) | Supported |
| Mobile browsers | Responsive layout, not primary use case |

### Responsive Design

- **Desktop (≥1024px):** 3–4 column card grid, full chart labels, summary stats
- **Tablet (768–1023px):** 2 column grid, condensed labels
- **Mobile (<768px):** Single column, charts stack vertically — usable but not primary
- Chart library must support container-responsive sizing via `autoSize: true`

### Performance Targets

| Target | Value |
|---|---|
| Cache load time | < 2s from local JSON |
| `fetch-data` script | < 60s for ~44 API calls + full.jsonl command scan |
| JS bundle | < 150KB gzipped |
| First contentful paint | < 1.5s (local dev server) |
| Chart render (20 charts) | < 500ms after data load |

### SEO Strategy

None required — internal demo, no public indexing.

### Accessibility Level

WCAG AA colour contrast on charts. Semantic HTML for chart containers. No screen reader requirement for v1 demo scope.

### Implementation Considerations

- Charts: reuse existing `lightweight-charts` for time-series; add lightweight bar/pie library (e.g., `Chart.js` or `unovis`) for non-time-series metrics
- Styling: extend existing Tailwind v4 `@theme` tokens — dark aesthetic already established
- All 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page — no routing needed
- Cache file gitignored — run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`

### Phase 2 — Growth (Post-Demo, if adopted internally)

- GitLab API support alongside GitHub API
- Multi-repo aggregation view
- Per-developer breakdown (requires multi-developer checkpoint data)
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, stop reason distribution
- CSV/report export

### Phase 3 — Expansion (Platform Vision)

- Real-time ingestion via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across developers and repos
- DORA-style AI benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal

### Risk Mitigation Strategy

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| `full.jsonl` files too large | Stream line-by-line; extract command strings only; skip on error |

| GitLab-only teams can't adopt | GitLab migration is Phase 2 priority; noted in architecture |

- All 20 charts/metrics populate without errors from real `entire/checkpoints/v1` data
- No runtime errors during screen recording

### Measurable Outcomes

| Outcome | Signal |
|---|---|
| Demo is self-explanatory | No verbal explanation needed for any chart |
| Approach is credible | Team agrees data is real, sourced from actual work sessions |
| Scope is sufficient | All 4 metric domains represented (attribution, cost, quality, behavioral) |
| Follow-up happens | At least one concrete next step agreed in the meeting |

## Product Scope

### MVP — v1 Demo Dashboard (20 metrics across all domains)

**Data tiers:**
- **Lightweight** (metrics 1–13, 15–20): sourced from `metadata.json` files only (~150KB total)
- **Heavy** (metric 14): targeted parse of `full.jsonl` user messages for command extraction only

**Attribution (5 metrics)**
1. Agent % per commit — trend line over all checkpoints sorted by `commit_date`
2. Pure-AI commit rate — % of checkpoints where `agent_percentage === 100`
3. Human modification rate — `human_modified` lines as % of `agent_lines` per checkpoint
4. Agent vs. human lines — stacked bar per checkpoint (agent_lines / human_added / human_modified / human_removed)
5. Zero-human-touch lines total — cumulative `agent_lines` where all human attribution fields = 0

**Token Economics (4 metrics)**
6. Token breakdown per checkpoint — stacked: input / cache_creation / cache_read / output
7. Cache leverage score — `cache_read_tokens / total_tokens` per checkpoint, trended
8. Total API call count — per checkpoint bar chart
9. Model distribution — pie/donut of Claude models (`model` field) across all turns

**Quality Signals (4 metrics)**
10. Friction events per checkpoint — `summary.friction` array length, bar chart
11. Open items debt — `summary.open_items` array length per checkpoint, trend line
12. Learnings generated — sum of `learnings.repo + learnings.code + learnings.workflow` counts per checkpoint
13. Session depth — `session_metrics.turn_count` per turn, scatter against `agent_percentage`

**Behavioral / SDLC (4 metrics)**
14. BMAD command usage frequency — parse `type: "user"` messages in `full.jsonl` across all turns, extract all `/bmad-*` and `/*` command patterns, count and rank by frequency. Stored in cache as extracted command strings only. Source: heavy tier (`full.jsonl`)
15. Prompt type distribution — classify `prompt.txt` per turn as: slash command / free-form / continuation (empty), pie chart
16. Files touched per checkpoint — count, coloured by file layer (`components/`, `services/`, `stores/`, `utils/`, docs)
17. Checkpoints per session — group turns by `session_id`, show how many checkpoints each session spans

**Temporal (3 metrics)**
18. Commit activity timeline — all checkpoints on a time axis by `commit_date`, coloured by `agent_percentage`
19. Checkpoint cadence — time delta between consecutive commits in minutes, bar chart
20. Attribution trend over project — `agent_percentage` rolling average across all commits chronologically

### Growth Features (Post-MVP)

- Configurable repo/branch via UI or `.env`
- GitLab API support
- Multi-repo aggregation
- Per-developer breakdown
- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, hook failure rate, stop reason distribution
- Export to CSV / shareable report

### Vision (Future)

- Real-time ingestion pipeline via webhook on checkpoint branch push
- Team comparison dashboard across multiple developers and repos
- DORA-style AI metrics benchmark against aggregated data
- Integration into internal developer portal

## User Journeys

### Journey 1: The Presenter — Setting Up and Recording the Demo

**Persona:** Dmytro, developer evaluating AI observability tooling for his team. Has done research, needs a compelling recorded demo for a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** Day before the meeting. Runs `npm run fetch-data` — script walks the GitHub API checkpoint tree, fetches ~44 metadata files, parses `full.jsonl` for BMAD command patterns, writes `checkpoints-cache.json` in under 60 seconds.

**Rising Action:** Runs `npm run dev`, dashboard populates instantly from cache. Attribution trend shows arc from 60% to 100% agent-written code. BMAD command chart shows `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`. Token costs visible per checkpoint.

**Climax:** Screen recording — 4-minute walkthrough: attribution ("how much AI"), token economics ("what it cost"), quality signals ("where friction occurred"), BMAD commands ("which skills, how often").

**Resolution:** Concrete, data-driven artifact that needs no live infrastructure — just a browser and a video file.

**Capabilities revealed:** fetch script, local cache, static dashboard, all 20 metric charts, temporal ordering by commit date.

---

### Journey 2: The Viewer — Engineering Lead Evaluating the Approach

**Persona:** Senior engineering lead, skeptical of new tooling, interested in AI observability. Watches the recorded demo in a team meeting.

**Opening Scene:** First question: *"Is this real data or a mock?"* Confirmed — real project, real commits, real sessions.

**Rising Action:** Attribution trend lands. *"That 100% agent line — zero human edits on that commit?"* Yes. *"Which files were touched?"* Files-by-layer chart answers it. *"Can we see if AI touches services differently than components?"* Yes.

**Climax:** BMAD command frequency chart appears. `/bmad-dev-story ×8`, `/bmad-code-review ×6`, `/bmad-create-story ×3`. *"This shows exactly how the developer structured their AI workflow — not just that they used AI, but how."* The approach clicks.

**Resolution:** *"Can we run this against our own repos?"* — the success signal. Team agrees to evaluate Entire as a platform.

**Capabilities revealed:** data credibility indicator, commit date ordering, files-by-layer chart, BMAD command breakdown, clear chart labels.

---

### Journey 3: The Sceptic — Developer Who Questions the Data

**Persona:** Developer on the team concerned that AI metrics could be used to evaluate individual performance.

**Opening Scene:** Sees attribution charts. *"Is this going to be used to judge how much AI someone uses?"*

**Rising Action:** Dashboard shows aggregate patterns — no developer names, no performance ranking. Quality signals show where the workflow struggled, not who struggled.

**Resolution:** The data model itself answers the trust question — session-level context only, no PII, no individual performance ranking.

**Capabilities revealed:** no PII/developer identification in v1, aggregate-only view, metrics framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs.

---

### Journey Requirements Summary

| Capability | Revealed by Journey |
|---|---|
| `npm run fetch-data` script | J1 |
| Local JSON cache, sub-2s load | J1 |
| All 20 metric charts | J1, J2 |
| Commit date ordering / temporal axis | J1, J2 |
| Files touched by layer chart | J2 |
| BMAD command frequency chart | J2 |
| Clear chart labels, no jargon | J2 |
| No PII, aggregate-only model | J3 |
| Data source credibility indicator | J2, J3 |

## Innovation & Novel Patterns

### Detected Innovation Areas

**1. Git as AI Observability Store**
Entire repurposes Git — already universal in engineering teams — as the persistence layer for AI session telemetry. This dashboard is the first layer that makes that data legible. The innovation is not in the dashboard itself but in the data model it reads: structured, versioned, permanently associated with the commit it produced.

**2. Zero-Infrastructure Observability**
Most observability tools require agents, sidecars, log shippers, or SaaS integrations. This approach requires none — the data pipeline is `git push`. The dashboard is a read layer on top of data that already exists. Category inversion: instead of "add instrumentation to capture AI usage," it's "read the instrumentation that AI commits already carry."

**3. Attribution at Commit Granularity**
Existing tools (GitHub Copilot metrics, IDE plugins) report AI usage at session or daily level. Entire captures attribution per commit — `agent_lines` vs `human_modified` vs `human_removed` per checkpoint. This dashboard is the first visualisation layer for that granularity.

**4. Workflow Intelligence from Prompt Patterns**
Parsing `/bmad-*` command invocations from user messages surfaces *how* AI was directed, not just *that* it was used. New signal category: AI workflow structure, visible in aggregate across a project's history.

### Validation Approach

The PoC itself is the validation — real data from a real project (`entire/checkpoints/v1`) running through the pipeline. Secondary validation: the team asks "can we run this on our own repos?" — that question confirms the approach generalises.

### Risk Mitigation

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Sparse checkpoint data | Demo uses a completed project with 11 checkpoints — sufficient density |
| GitHub API rate limits | ~44 calls, within unauthenticated 60/hour limit; token optional |
| GitLab migration breaks approach | Abstract fetch layer; GitHub/GitLab interchangeable at API level |
| Team perceives as surveillance | No PII, aggregate-only, framed as workflow signals not performance KPIs |

## Web App Specific Requirements

### Project-Type Overview

Static single-page application built with Astro. All data loaded from a local JSON cache at build time. No backend, no authentication, no server-side rendering. Dashboard viewed on desktop during demo recording, designed responsively for any team member who opens it in a browser.

### Technical Architecture Considerations

**Build Pipeline:**
```
npm run fetch-data
  → scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts
  → src/data/checkpoints-cache.json (local, gitignored)

npm run dev / build
  → Astro reads cache at build time
  → client-side chart rendering
```

**Fetch Script Targets:**
- `GET /git/trees/{branch}?recursive=1` — discover all checkpoint paths
- `GET /contents/{path}/metadata.json` × ~41 files — parent + turn metadata
- `GET /commits?sha={branch}` — commit dates and checkpoint_id mapping
- Targeted `full.jsonl` parse — scan `type:"user"` messages for `/bmad-*` commands only

### Browser Matrix

| Browser | Support |
|---|---|
| Chrome (latest) | Primary — demo recording target |
| Firefox (latest) | Supported |
| Safari (latest) | Supported |
| Edge (latest) | Supported |
| Mobile browsers | Responsive layout, not primary use case |

### Responsive Design

- **Desktop (≥1024px):** 3–4 column card grid, full chart labels, summary stats
- **Tablet (768–1023px):** 2 column grid, condensed labels
- **Mobile (<768px):** Single column, charts stack vertically — usable but not primary
- Chart library must support container-responsive sizing via `autoSize: true`

### Performance Targets

| Target | Value |
|---|---|
| Cache load time | < 2s from local JSON |
| `fetch-data` script | < 60s for ~44 API calls + full.jsonl command scan |
| JS bundle | < 150KB gzipped |
| First contentful paint | < 1.5s (local dev server) |
| Chart render (20 charts) | < 500ms after data load |

### SEO Strategy

None required — internal demo, no public indexing.

### Accessibility Level

WCAG AA colour contrast on charts. Semantic HTML for chart containers. No screen reader requirement for v1 demo scope.

### Implementation Considerations

- Charts: reuse existing `lightweight-charts` for time-series; add lightweight bar/pie library (e.g., `Chart.js` or `unovis`) for non-time-series metrics
- Styling: extend existing Tailwind v4 `@theme` tokens — dark aesthetic already established
- All 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page — no routing needed
- Cache file gitignored — run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`

lines: - All 20 metrics on a single scrollable dashboard page — no routing needed
- Cache file gitignored — run `fetch-data` locally before `dev` or `build`
+## Project Scoping & Phased Development
+### MVP Strategy & Philosophy
+**MVP Approach:** Problem-solving PoC — demonstrate that Git-native AI observability is real, functional, and meaningful using actual checkpoint data from a completed project.
+**Resource Requirements:** 1 developer, ~1 sprint. No external dependencies beyond GitHub API (unauthenticated) and existing Astro repo infrastructure.
+### MVP Feature Set (Phase 1) — v1 Demo Dashboard
+**Core User Journeys Supported:**
+- Journey 1: Presenter runs fetch, records dashboard walkthrough
+- Journey 2: Viewer evaluates the approach from the recording
+- Journey 3: Sceptic's trust concern addressed by aggregate-only data model
+**Must-Have Capabilities:**
+| Capability | Justification |
+| `scripts/fetch-checkpoints.ts` | Entire data pipeline — nothing works without it |
+| `src/data/checkpoints-cache.json` schema | Contract between fetch script and dashboard |
+| 20 metric charts (all domains) | Each represents a distinct signal category for evaluation |
+| Temporal ordering by `commit_date` | Required for trend analysis |
+| BMAD command frequency (metric #14) | Key workflow intelligence signal — requires `full.jsonl` parse |
+| Responsive layout | Team members may open on different devices |
+| Data source credibility indicator | Addresses the "is this real?" question immediately |
+**Out of MVP Scope:**
+- Configurable repo/branch — hardcoded to `ShamsiievDmytro/entire-bmad`
+- Authentication — public repo, no token required for demo
+- Multiple pages or routing — single scrollable page only
+- Export / share features
+### Phase 2 — Growth (Post-Demo, if adopted internally)
+- `.env`-configurable repo owner, repo name, branch
+- GitLab API support alongside GitHub API
+- Multi-repo aggregation view
+- Per-developer breakdown (requires multi-developer checkpoint data)
+- `full.jsonl` deep analysis: tool call palette, thinking block ratio, stop reason distribution
+- CSV/report export
+### Phase 3 — Expansion (Platform Vision)
+- Real-time ingestion via webhook on checkpoint branch push
+- Team comparison dashboard across developers and repos
+- DORA-style AI benchmark against aggregated data
+- Integration into internal developer portal
+### Risk Mitigation Strategy
+| Risk | Mitigation |
+| `full.jsonl` files too large | Stream line-by-line; extract command strings only; skip on error |
+| GitHub API 60 req/hour limit | ~44 calls — within limit; optional `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var |
+| 20 charts feels like too many | Group into tabbed sections or collapsible domain panels |
+| Team asks for live data during meeting | Recorded walkthrough removes live dependency |
+| GitLab-only teams can't adopt | GitLab migration is Phase 2 priority; noted in architecture |
